---
title: "NGS Workflows with systemPipeR"
subtitle: "GEN242: Data Analysis in Genome Biology"
author: "Thomas Girke"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../assets/revealjs_custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    scrollable: true
    smaller: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-block-height: 340px
    transition: slide
    embed-resources: true
    footer: "GEN242 · UC Riverside · [systemPipeR Tutorial](https://girke.bioinformatics.ucr.edu/GEN242/tutorials/systempiper/systemPipeR_index.html) · [RNA-Seq Tutorial](https://girke.bioinformatics.ucr.edu/GEN242/tutorials/sprnaseq/sprnaseq_index.html)"
    logo: "https://girke.bioinformatics.ucr.edu/GEN242/assets/logo_gen242.png"
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

## Outline

1. **Motivation** — why a common NGS workflow environment?
2. **Design** — systemPipeR architecture and key concepts
3. **Getting started** — install, templates, targets file, CWL
4. **Running workflows** — single machine and cluster execution
5. **Workflow templates** — RNA-Seq, VAR-Seq, ChIP-Seq, Ribo-Seq
6. **RNA-Seq walkthrough** — 10 steps from raw reads to report
7. **Demonstration** — running a full RNA-Seq workflow

::: {.callout-note}
**Tutorials:**
[systemPipeR Workflow Framework](https://girke.bioinformatics.ucr.edu/GEN242/tutorials/systempiper/systemPipeR_index.html) ·
[RNA-Seq Workflow](https://girke.bioinformatics.ucr.edu/GEN242/tutorials/sprnaseq/sprnaseq_index.html)
:::

---

## Motivation — Many NGS Applications, Shared Needs {.scrollable}

### The diversity of NGS applications

Modern genomics produces data from many different assay types, each requiring
its own analysis pipeline:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Genome resequencing
- mRNA / miRNA profiling
- Methylome profiling
- De novo sequencing
- Splice variant analysis
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Functional element discovery
- ChIP-Seq (protein-DNA interactions)
- Hi-C (chromosome structure)
- Metagenome analysis
- Ribo-Seq (translation efficiency)
:::
::::

### Overlapping analysis steps across applications

Despite their differences, most NGS workflows share a common core:

| Step | Applies to |
|---|---|
| Read QC and preprocessing | all NGS |
| Read alignment to genome/transcriptome | most NGS |
| Feature quantification | RNA-Seq, ChIP-Seq, Ribo-Seq |
| Feature annotation | all NGS |
| Enrichment analysis | RNA-Seq, VAR-Seq, ChIP-Seq |

### Why a common workflow environment?

- **Efficiency** — build reusable components instead of re-implementing per project
- **Standardization** — uniform analysis steps improve comparability across experiments
- **Reproducibility** — complete, documented pipelines that others can re-run
- **Scale** — the same code handles 1 sample or 1,000 samples on a cluster

---

## Requirements for a Workflow Environment

A robust NGS workflow environment must support:

- **End-to-end pipelines** — from raw reads to final biological results
- **Single machine and HPC clusters** — via queuing systems (Slurm, SGE)
- **Any software** — command-line tools (HISAT2, STAR, samtools) and R functions
- **Automated report generation** — HTML (R Markdown), PDF (LaTeX), notebooks
- **Uniform sample handling** — minimum human intervention for large sample sets
- **Flexibility** — easy to customize and add new tools without redesigning the framework
- **Scalability and portability** — runs in RStudio, JupyterHub, SSH terminal, cloud

### Run environments

![HPCC cluster run environments — RStudio Server, JupyterHub, and SSH terminal all connect to the same compute cluster and storage backend.](images/run_environments.png){width=75%}

---

## systemPipeR — Design Overview {.scrollable}

`systemPipeR` is an R/Bioconductor package providing a complete NGS workflow
and report generation environment.

### Architecture

![systemPipeR workflow design overview — sample metadata flows from a targets file through CWL parameter files into SYSargsList steps, which are executed on single machines or clusters.](images/systemPipeR_design.png){width=85%}

### Key components

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Targets file** | defines all input files and sample metadata |
| **CWL `.cwl` file** | specifies command-line tool settings |
| **CWL `.yml` file** | maps targets variables to tool parameters |
| **`SYSargsList`** | the central workflow object — holds all steps |
| **`runWF()`** | executes the workflow on local machine or cluster |
| **`plotWF()`** | visualizes workflow topology as a DAG |
| **`renderReport()`** | generates HTML analysis report |

### Common Workflow Language (CWL) integration

CWL is a community standard for describing computational workflows:

- Provides **portability** across platforms, schedulers, and languages
- Workflows can run from R, command-line (`cwl-runner`), or other languages
- Supports **containerization** (Docker, Singularity), HPC clusters, and cloud
- Language-agnostic — tools written in any language can be integrated

---

## Getting Started — Install and Load {.scrollable}

### Install required packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly=TRUE))
    install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install(c("systemPipeR", "systemPipeRdata"))
```
:::


### Load packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library("systemPipeR")
library("systemPipeRdata")  # provides workflow templates and sample data
```
:::


### Getting help


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(help="systemPipeR")   # list all functions
vignette("systemPipeR")       # open the main vignette
```
:::


### Generate a workflow template

`systemPipeRdata` generates pre-configured workflow environments with a single
command. Available templates: `rnaseq`, `varseq`, `chipseq`, `riboseq`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# From within R
genWorkenvir(workflow="rnaseq", mydirname=NULL, bam=FALSE)
setwd("rnaseq")
```
:::


```bash
# Command-line alternative
Rscript -e "systemPipeRdata::genWorkenvir(workflow='rnaseq')"
```

### Workflow directory structure

```default
rnaseq/              # workflow .Rmd script, targets file
  param/             # CWL parameter files (.cwl and .yml) for each tool
  data/              # input files: FASTQ reads, reference genome, annotations
  results/           # output files generated during the workflow run
```

---

## The Targets File — Sample Metadata {.scrollable}

The **targets file** defines all input samples and their metadata. It is the
single source of truth for sample identity throughout the workflow.

### Single-end (SE) library format


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
read.delim("targets.txt", comment.char="#")[1:3, 1:5]
```
:::


```default
               FileName SampleName Factor SampleLong Experiment
1 ./data/SRR446027_1.fastq        M1A     M1  Mock.1h.A          1
2 ./data/SRR446028_1.fastq        M1B     M1  Mock.1h.B          1
3 ./data/SRR446029_1.fastq        A1A     A1   Avr.1h.A          1
```

### Paired-end (PE) library format


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
read.delim("targetsPE.txt", comment.char="#")[1:3, 1:4]
```
:::


```default
              FileName1                FileName2 SampleName Factor
1 ./data/SRR446027_1.fastq ./data/SRR446027_2.fastq        M1A     M1
2 ./data/SRR446028_1.fastq ./data/SRR446028_2.fastq        M1B     M1
3 ./data/SRR446029_1.fastq ./data/SRR446029_2.fastq        A1A     A1
```

::: {.callout-tip}
A sample targets file is available in Google Sheets format.
It can be imported as a tab-delimited text file (preferred) or directly via
the `googlesheets4` package:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(googlesheets4)
gs4_deauth()   # no login needed for public sheets
df <- as.data.frame(
    read_sheet("1U-32UcwZP1k3saKeaH1mbvEAOfZRdNHNkWK2GI1rpPM", skip=4)
)
```
:::

:::

### What targets file columns define

| Column | Content |
|---|---|
| `FileName` / `FileName1` / `FileName2` | input FASTQ file paths |
| `SampleName` | unique sample identifier (used in output filenames) |
| `Factor` | experimental group for DEG comparisons |
| `SampleLong` | longer descriptive name |
| `Experiment` | batch or experiment number |

---

## SYSargsList — Project Initialization and Steps {.scrollable}

### Initialize a workflow project


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- SPRproject()
# Creating directory '.SPRproject'
# Creating file '.SPRproject/SYSargsList.yml'
```
:::


### Populate with steps from a workflow Rmd


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- importWF(sal, file_path="my_workflow.Rmd")
sal   # inspect the workflow object
```
:::


### Three types of code chunks in workflow `.Rmd` files

| Type | Declaration | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Regular R chunks | standard `{r}` | instructions, learning examples — not part of workflow |
| R workflow steps | `spr=TRUE` + `LineWise({...})` | R code executed as workflow steps |
| Command-line steps | `SYSargsList()` constructor | runs external tools (HISAT2, samtools, etc.) |

### Add a command-line step manually


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
appendStep(sal) <- SYSargsList(
    step_name  = "hisat2_mapping",
    dir        = TRUE,
    targets    = "targetsPE.txt",
    wf_file    = "workflow-hisat2/workflow_hisat2-pe.cwl",
    input_file = "workflow-hisat2/workflow_hisat2-pe.yml",
    dir_path   = "param/cwl",
    inputvars  = c(FileName1   = "_FASTQ_PATH1_",
                   FileName2   = "_FASTQ_PATH2_",
                   SampleName  = "_SampleName_")
)
```
:::


**From targets → yml → cwl:**

- `targets.txt` → sample metadata (FileName, SampleName, Factor)
- `.yml` file → maps targets variables to tool parameter placeholders
- `.cwl` file → defines the actual command-line tool invocation

---

## SYSargsList — Inspect, Run and Visualize {.scrollable}

### Inspect command-line calls before running

`cmdlist()` shows the exact shell command that will be executed for each sample:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cmdlist(sal, step="hisat2_mapping", targets=1)
```
:::


```default
$hisat2_mapping$M1A$hisat2
[1] "hisat2 -S ./results/M1A.sam -x ./data/tair10.fasta
        -1 ./data/SRR446027_1.fastq.gz -2 ./data/SRR446027_2.fastq.gz --threads 4"
$hisat2_mapping$M1A$samtools-view
[1] "samtools view -bS -o ./results/M1A.bam ./results/M1A.sam"
$hisat2_mapping$M1A$samtools-sort
[1] "samtools sort -o ./results/M1A.sorted.bam ./results/M1A.bam -@ 4"
$hisat2_mapping$M1A$samtools-index
[1] "samtools index -b results/M1A.sorted.bam results/M1A.sorted.bam.bai"
```

### Inspect expected input and output files


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
outfiles(sal)[["hisat2_mapping"]][1:2,]
targetsWF(sal)[["hisat2_mapping"]][1:2, 1:4]
```
:::


### Run on a single machine


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- runWF(sal)
```
:::


### Submit to a cluster (Slurm)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- addResources(sal, "hisat2_mapping", resources=resources)
sal <- runWF(sal)   # same call — resources argument triggers cluster submission
```
:::


### Visualize workflow topology


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plotWF(sal)   # renders an interactive DAG showing step dependencies
```
:::


### Generate analysis reports and logs


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
rmarkdown::render("mywf.Rmd")   # full workflow report
sal <- renderReport(sal)         # same via sal object
sal <- renderLogs(sal)           # logs for each step
```
:::


---

## Workflow Templates — Available Pipelines {.scrollable}

`systemPipeRdata` provides pre-built templates for the most common NGS workflows.
All follow the same design — only the tools and parameters differ.

### RNA-Seq

Read preprocessing → QC report → Alignment (HISAT2, STAR, Kallisto) → Alignment stats → Read counting → Sample correlation → DEG analysis (edgeR, DESeq2) → GO enrichment → Gene cluster analysis

### VAR-Seq (variant calling)

Read preprocessing → QC → Alignment (gsnap, bwa) → Alignment stats → Variant calling (VariantTools, GATK, BCFtools) → Variant filtering → Variant annotation → Multi-sample summary

### ChIP-Seq

Read preprocessing → QC → Alignment (Bowtie2, rsubread) → Alignment stats → Genome-wide coverage → Peak calling (MACS2, BayesPeak) → Peak annotation → Differential binding → GO enrichment → Motif analysis

### Ribo-Seq

Adaptor trimming + QC → Alignment → Read distribution across features → Custom feature addition (e.g. uORF prediction) → Coverage along transcripts → Read counting → Sample correlation → DEG analysis → GO enrichment → Cluster analysis → Differential ribosome binding (translational efficiency)

::: {.callout-tip}
Generate any template with one command:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
genWorkenvir(workflow="rnaseq")   # or "varseq", "chipseq", "riboseq"
```
:::

Each template includes sample data for testing the complete pipeline.
:::

---

## RNA-Seq Workflow — 10-Step Overview {.scrollable}

The RNA-Seq template implements a complete analysis from raw reads to
biological interpretation. All steps run with `runWF(sal)`.

### Step 1 — Read preprocessing

- Demultiplexing, adaptor trimming, quality filtering, duplicate removal
- Largely based on `ShortRead` utilities

### Step 2 — FASTQ quality report

- Per-position base quality, nucleotide frequency, read length distribution, k-mer analysis
- Generated with `seeFastqPlot()` from `systemPipeR`

### Step 3 — Read alignment

```default
FileName Nreads2x Nalign Perc_Aligned Nalign_Primary Perc_Aligned_Primary
     M1A   192918 177961     92.25           177961               92.25
     M1B   197484 159378     80.70           159378               80.70
     A1A   189870 176055     92.72           176055               92.72
```

- Supported aligners: HISAT2, Bowtie2/Tophat2, STAR, Rsubread, Kallisto (via tximport)
- Alignment stats generated with `alignStats()`

### Step 4 — Read counting

- Count reads per genomic feature (exonic gene ranges for RNA-Seq)
- Strand-sensitive and insensitive modes
- Fully parallelized via `summarizeOverlaps()`

```default
          M1A M1B  A1A  A1B  V1A  V1B ...
AT1G01010  28 254  203  183  371  233 ...
AT1G01020  16 104   74  130  111   90 ...
AT1G01030  20 108   74   60   99  159 ...
```

### Step 5 — Feature count QC plots

- Library size normalization
- Per-sample count distributions
- Identifies outlier samples before downstream analysis

### Step 6 — Sample-wise correlation (tree / MDS)

- Hierarchical clustering tree and MDS/PCA plots
- Identifies batch effects and groups biological replicates

### Step 7 — DEG analysis

- Fully automated for pairwise designs
- Supports **edgeR** and **DESeq2**
- Volcano plots, MA plots, DEG tables

### Step 8 — GO term enrichment analysis

- Automated for large numbers of DEG sets or clusters
- Full and slim GO analysis

### Step 9 — Gene-wise cluster analysis

- Clusters genes with similar expression profiles across conditions
- Heatmaps, k-means, hierarchical clustering

### Step 10 — Analysis report

- Auto-generated HTML or PDF report
- Includes all code with syntax highlighting, figures, and tables
- In-text evaluation of variables (counts, p-values, etc.)
- Human-generated text with citations integrated via R Markdown / knitr


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- renderReport(sal)         # generates complete HTML report
```
:::


::: {.callout-note}
All report functionalities are essential for **reproducible research** — the
report is a living document that can be regenerated from scratch from the raw data.
:::

---

## RNA-Seq Demonstration {.scrollable}

A complete RNA-Seq workflow can be executed with just a few commands.

### Step-by-step execution on HPCC cluster

**1. Populate the targets file**

Edit `targets.txt` with your sample FASTQ file paths and metadata.
A template is [available here](https://girke.bioinformatics.ucr.edu/GEN242/tutorials/sprnaseq/sprnaseq_index.html).

**2. Generate the workflow environment**


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
genWorkenvir(workflow="rnaseq")
setwd("rnaseq")
```
:::


**3. Initialize and run the complete workflow**


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- SPRproject()
sal <- importWF(sal, file_path="systemPipeRNAseq.Rmd")
sal <- runWF(sal)      # runs all steps; submits cluster jobs automatically
```
:::


**4. Monitor cluster jobs**

```bash
squeue              # from command line
# or
jobMonitor          # custom HPCC visualization tool
```

**5. Render the final report**


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sal <- renderReport(sal)
```
:::


Open the HTML report in your browser. View alignment results interactively
in [IGV](https://igv.org/) (example region: `Chr1:49,457–51,457`).

---

## Summary

| Component | Command / Concept |
|---|---|
| **Install** | `BiocManager::install(c("systemPipeR", "systemPipeRdata"))` |
| **Template** | `genWorkenvir(workflow="rnaseq")` |
| **Initialize** | `sal <- SPRproject()` |
| **Import workflow** | `sal <- importWF(sal, file_path="workflow.Rmd")` |
| **Inspect calls** | `cmdlist(sal, step="step_name")` |
| **Run locally** | `sal <- runWF(sal)` |
| **Run on cluster** | `sal <- addResources(sal, "step", resources)` then `runWF(sal)` |
| **Visualize** | `plotWF(sal)` |
| **Report** | `sal <- renderReport(sal)` |
| **Targets file** | defines all samples and input file paths |
| **CWL `.cwl`** | command-line tool settings |
| **CWL `.yml`** | maps targets variables to tool parameters |

### References

- Backman T and Girke T (2016) systemPipeR: NGS workflow and report generation environment. *BMC Bioinformatics* 17: 1–8. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12859-016-1241-0)
- Huber W, et al. (2015) Orchestrating high-throughput genomic analysis with Bioconductor. *Nat Methods* 12: 115–121. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1038/nmeth.3252)
- [systemPipeR Bioconductor page](https://bioconductor.org/packages/systemPipeR)
- [CWL Website](https://www.commonwl.org/)
- [HPCC Cluster Manual](https://hpcc.ucr.edu)

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
